coredata.dc:title: Carbonized wood loaded with carbon dots for preparation long-term shape-stabilized composite phase change materials with superior thermal energy conversion capacity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xinghui
Zhu, Ziqi
Yang, Pei
You, Zhenping
Dong, Yue
Tang, Miao
Chen, Minzhi
Zhou, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: The leakage issue, low thermal conductivity and low photo-thermal conversion capacity of organic phase change material (PCMs) greatly limits their practical utilization. Herein, a novel shape-stabilized composite PCMs with enhanced thermal conductivity and superior photo-thermal conversion efficiency was fabricated by incorporating polyethylene glycol (PEG) into porous carbonized wood loaded with carbon dots (CDs). The CDs-contained supporting material was not only characterized by unique three-dimensional porous structure, but also beneficial to prevent the leakage of melted PEG during the phase change process. The underlying reason was that extra hydrogen-bonding interaction between CDs and PEG can be readily formed in addition to surface tension, capillary action. Thus, the constructed PCMs outperformed the one without CDs incorporation in terms of shape-stabilized performance. Further, the CDs-contained PCMs exhibited high thermal conductivity (0.449 W/m K), which was comparable to the PCMs using carbonized wood as single supporting material, but showing 117% higher than that of pristine PEG. More importantly, this novel supporting material fabricated by integrating carbonized wood and CDs also imparted the resultant PCMs with high photo-thermal conversion efficiency (84.4%), latent heat (112.4 J/g), thermal reliability and thermal stability, thus demonstrating great potential utilization in constructing solar water heating systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change materials
Polyethylene glycol
Carbonized wood
Carbon dots
Photo-thermal conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and chemical structural of CDs and CW-CDs
Phase change performances of composite PCMs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of the CW-CDs-PEG, and the process of light-to-heat conversion and storage.

(a) TEM image of the CDs. (b) Particle size distribution of CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (d) XPS survey spectrum of CW. (e) High-resolution XPS of C 1s of CW. (f) XPS survey spectrum and (g–i) High-resolution XPS of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra of CW-CDs.

SEM images with different magnifications of cross section and longitudinal section of CW, CW-CDs, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG. (a1, a2) Cross section and (a3) longitudinal section of CW, respectively. (b1, b2) Cross section and (b3) longitudinal section of CW-CDs, respectively. (c1, c2) Cross section and (c3) longitudinal section of CW-PEG, respectively. (d1, d2) Cross section and (d3) longitudinal section of CW-CDs-PEG, respectively. The porous structure of CW and CW-CDs were filled with PEG.
The photographs of (a) pure PEG4000, (b) CW-PEG and (c) CW-CDs-PEG were heated at 80 °C for 6 h.

(a) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of CW and CW-CDs. (b) The pore size distribution of CW and CW-CDs. (c) The XRD patterns of pure PEG, CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG. (d) The FTIR spectra of pure PEG, CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG.

(a) DSC curves of Pure PEG, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG. The DSC curves for melting and cooling of (b) CW-PEG and (c) CW-CDs-PEG after 50 cycles. (d) Comparison of the malting and cooling enthalpies of Pure PEG, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG, CW-PEG-50, and CW-CDs-PEG-50.
Thermal conductivity of pure PEG, CW, CW-CDs, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG.
(a) TGA and corresponding (b) DTG curves of pure PEG, CW, CW-CDs, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG.

(a) Experimental setup of the photo-thermal energy transformation measurement device. (b) Temperature variation plots of PEG, CW, CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG with or without the simulated solar illumination (xenon lamp). (c, d) Infrared thermography photos show the surface temperature variation of PEG, CW, CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG at same heating and cooling times, respectively. (e) The surface temperature of PEG, CW, CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG was completely cooled to room temperature, respectively.
Some wood-based composite phase change materials.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Time: Leakage test time; T.C.: Thermal conductivity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Phase change characteristics of pure PEG, CW-PEG, CW-CDs-PEG, CW-PEG-50, and CW-CDs-PEG-50 during the melting crystallization processes.

represent the latent heat of fusion the composite PCMs and pure PEG, respectively.

head.ce:title: Carbonized wood loaded with carbon dots for preparation long-term shape-stabilized composite phase change materials with superior thermal energy conversion capacity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The leakage issue, low thermal conductivity and low photo-thermal conversion capacity of organic phase change material (PCMs) greatly limits their practical utilization. Herein, a novel shape-stabilized composite PCMs with enhanced thermal conductivity and superior photo-thermal conversion efficiency was fabricated by incorporating polyethylene glycol (PEG) into porous carbonized wood loaded with carbon dots (CDs). The CDs-contained supporting material was not only characterized by unique three-dimensional porous structure, but also beneficial to prevent the leakage of melted PEG during the phase change process. The underlying reason was that extra hydrogen-bonding interaction between CDs and PEG can be readily formed in addition to surface tension, capillary action. Thus, the constructed PCMs outperformed the one without CDs incorporation in terms of shape-stabilized performance. Further, the CDs-contained PCMs exhibited high thermal conductivity (0.449 W/m K), which was comparable to the PCMs using carbonized wood as single supporting material, but showing 117% higher than that of pristine PEG. More importantly, this novel supporting material fabricated by integrating carbonized wood and CDs also imparted the resultant PCMs with high photo-thermal conversion efficiency (84.4%), latent heat (112.4 J/g), thermal reliability and thermal stability, thus demonstrating great potential utilization in constructing solar water heating systems.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increase of energy consumption and environment pollution, efficient energy conversion and storage techniques have attracted widespread attention. Thermal energy storage (TES) can effectively improve energy utilization and accomplish the sustainable control via sensible heat, latent heat and thermochemical reactions [1]. In particular, the use of the latent heat storage by phase change materials (PCMs) is an efficient approach, because PCMs had excellent good thermal stability and large storage capacities. Based on this unique thermal performance, PCMs thus far have been widely used in solar energy harvesting systems, air-conditioning systems, energy-saving buildings and clothing systems, and so on [2,3]. With the constant-depth study, the organic PCMs, such as polyethylene glycol (PEG), paraffins, and fatty acids, have been considered to be superior in a low-temperature TES over the inorganic PCMs (salt hydrates, molten salts, metal, and alloys) by their virtues of narrow phase change temperature, no phase segregation, a high thermal energy density, non-toxicity, no or little supercooling [4]. However, the practical application of organic PCMs is extremely restricted by the problems of the liquid leakage and low thermal conductivity, thus it is necessary to prepare shape-stabilized and thermally enhanced composite PCMs.

To address the leakage issue, form-stable PCMs (FSPCMs) or shape-stabilized PCMs (SSPCMs), which are composed by PCMs and supporting material, have become a research hotspot in recent years. Generally, there are basically three approaches including microencapsulation, porous absorption, and polymer matrix being devised to prepare FSPCMs or SSPCMs [5,6]. Despite great efforts undertaken by previous research, microencapsulated technique and polymer matrix have many limitations such as high cost of process and challenging work of selecting and designing correct materials according to the specific application [7,8]. With respect to the above-mentioned two strategies, porous absorption using expanded graphite oxide, metallic foams, porous scaffolds, carbon nanotube sponges and other porous minerals has been commonly regarded to be an efficient way for constructing PCMs due to their high porosity, and wide pore size distribution. Generally, expanded graphite oxide, metallic foams, porous scaffolds, carbon nanotube sponges and other porous minerals as the supporting material of PCMs [9]. Nevertheless, considering the high cost of these materials, increasing researches have focused on the preparation of low-cost renewable supporting materials by facile carbonization of biomass, and the developed precursors include winter melon, potatoes, eggplant, pumpkin, succulents and cotton [10–12]. These biomass-based 3D porous carbon material can effectively prevent the leakage of PCMs through the pore confinement and capillary force during the phase change process. However, it is still controversial that the composite PCMs with long-term stable shape can be formed by means of capillary force and carbonization structure. In addition to the natural porosity, wood is commonly characterized by virtues of sustainable, renewable and environmentally friendly, thus, wood-derived materials provide a new candidate as the precursor of supporting scaffold for fabricating shape-stabilized PCMs (as shown in Table 1 ). For instance, Yang et al. [13–16] prepared a series of multifunctional SSPCMs using de-lignified wood as supporting material, and the resultant composite PCMs exhibited superior comprehensive performances. Similarly, other researchers reported that the wood flour and carbonized wood with developed porous structures were also promising supporting materials for SSPCMs [17–20].

In addition to the leakage issue, enhancing the thermal conductivity of organic PCMs is another critical challenge [21,22]. In general, adding fillers with high thermal conductivity is a facile strategy to combat the aforementioned problem, and thus, in more recent years, carbon nanotubes, carbon fiber, graphene and metal nanoparticle have been incorporated into composite PCMs to improve the heat transfer [23–25]. However, these fillers suffer some internal shortcomings, including high-cost, low yield and environmentally friendliness. Hence, some researchers have focused on enhancing the thermal conductivity of the supporting materials by directly high-temperature carbonization, because the high degree of graphitization is commonly able to facilitate phonon transport and thus further benefit to the heat exchange [26]. Carbonized wood (CW) not only can inherit the cellular structure of the natural wood for the encapsulation of PCMs, but is also conducive to thermal conductivity and light absorption of the composite PCMs [27,28]. Whereas the problems of shape stability and photo-thermal conversion ability are not discussed in most of reports on carbonized wood-based composite PCMs. Also, although the three-dimensional porous structure of CW can prevent the melting leakage of PCMs in a short time, keeping long-term shape stability is still a challenge. Moreover, most of PCMs suffered low utilization efficiency of visible-light (45% of solar radiation). Therefore, further studies on the synchronous enhancement of the photo-thermal conversion capacity and thermal conductivity of composite PCMs are still necessary. Carbon dots (CDs) are commonly possessed of multiple functional groups, which is beneficial to form interaction with other materials and afford unexpected performances of CDs-contained composites, thus the CDs-contained carbonized wood as supporting materials will afford novel alternatives for constructing composite PCMs with shape-stability and excellent thermal performances.

Herein, a novel composite PCMs with shape-stabilized, enhanced thermal transport performance and improved thermal conversion capacity was fabricated by impregnating PEG into the CW loaded with CDs (CW-CDs), as shown in Fig. 1 . It should be mentioned that both the porous CW and CDs were prepared from biomass, making the fabricated supporting material with superiorities of simple preparation, low-cost and environmentally friendly. On the other hand, the prepared composite PCMs can take advantages of the phase change properties of PEG to absorb environmental heat or release heat into the environment, thus favoring the efficient utilization of solar energy for adjusting ambient temperature. In addition to the aforementioned excellent environmental features, the prepared PCMs composite demonstrated unique superiority. Specifically, not only the CW-CDs possessed hierarchical porous structure as similar as wood, but also the incorporated nano-sized CDs with multiple functional groups were able to prevent liquid leakage of PEG during the phase change process. Therefore, the CW-CDs-PEG exhibited excellent shape-stabilized performance when the CW-CDs was chosen as the supporting material. In addition, the CW-CDs-PEG possessed enhanced thermal conductivity (0.449 W/m K) compared with pure PEG (0.207 W/m K), the photo-thermal conversion efficiency of CW-CDs-PEG was found to be as high as 84.4%. Moreover, the latent heat, thermal reliability and stability of CW-CDs-PEG were also desirable. Hence, the outcomes of this work will not only expand the applications of low-cost biomass materials, but also provide a facile strategy for efficient utilization of solar energy.

Poplar woods were obtained from Huaian wood products Co., Ltd. Polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mn = 4000) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. D-(+)-Xylose and polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mn = 1800) were supported by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol was obtained from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

The bulk wood was firstly cut into blocks with the same size (2 × 2 × 0.5 cm3) for further treatment, and then the pristine wood samples were ultrasonically cleaned with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water successively followed by drying at 70 °C for 24 h, the dried samples were placed in a tube furnace for carbonization under the protection of nitrogen by programmed temperature controlling. Specifically, the temperature was slowly heated up to 250 °C at a heating rate of 3 °C/min, after incubating for 2 h, the temperature was raised to 1000 °C at 5 °C/min and then maintained for 2 h. The carbonized woods were thus obtained and labeled as CW.

The carbon dots (CDs) were prepared based on our previously reported method [29]. 0.5 g xylose and 0.8 g PEI were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. The solution formed after intensely stirring for tens of seconds. Afterward, the as-prepared solution was transferred into a reaction tube and heated in the microwave reactor at 220 °C and an input power of 200 W within just 10 min. After the reaction finished, the dark brown solution was obtained, and followed by cooling down to room temperature naturally. At last, the product was filtered by a PTFE syringe filter with pore size of 0.22 μm to remove the possible large particles. Then the solution was dialyzed with a dialysis membrane for 48 h to remove the unreacted small molecules.

Briefly, the prepared CW sample was immersed into the aqueous CDs solution (3.8 mg/mL) under vacuum at room temperature for 30 min to remove air. After the vacuum release, CDs filled the porous structure by atmosphere pressure. This process was repeated three times to ensure sufficient impregnation. Ultimately, the impregnated sample was pre-dried to a constant weight in an oven at 60 °C for 16 h, as shown in Fig. 1 (step 2).

The CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG were prepared by a vacuum impregnation method. Firstly, PEG was melted in a beaker at 80 °C. Thereafter, CW and CW-CDs were completely immersed into the same amount of liquid PEG, respectively, followed by vacuum adsorption at 60 °C with a period of 10 h. Subsequently, the residual liquid PEG on the surface of specimens was removed using filter paper. Samples of PEG infiltrated CW and CW-CDs were denoted as CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG composite respectively, as shown in Fig. 1 (step 3).

The micromorphology of specimens was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta 200, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV, and all samples surface were coated with a thin film of platinum before imaging to enhance the imaging contrast. The microstructure of CDs was observed using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 UHR, Japan). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Japan) with an Al Kα radiation was used to measure the chemical functional groups and atomic compositions of samples at room temperature. The chemical composition was determined by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR, Vertex, Germany) in the form of KBr pellet over the range of 400–4000 cm−1 and at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The crystal structure was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Ultima IV, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation at a tube current of 30 mA, scanning was at a speed of 5°/min the 2θ range of 5–65°. The specific surface area and pore size of carbon supporting materials were evaluated by a N2 adsorption/desorption analyzer (Autosorb-iQ) using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, while the pore size distribution was calculated according to the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) formula applied to the adsorption branch. Besides, the phase change behavior data including phase transition enthalpy and temperature were obtained by a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, 2003 F3, Germany) from −10 °C to 110 °C at 10 °C/min under nitrogen with a flow rate of 20 mL/min. The weight loss and thermal stability of specimens were evaluated by the thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, 209 F3, Germany). The samples were heated from 30 °C to 700 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 protection. The thermal conductivities were conducted by a thermal constant analyzer (Hot Disk, TPS2500S, Sweden) at room temperature, the transient plane source method was adopted as the measurement method. The samples were place on a heating sheet and continuously heated from room temperature to 100 °C, and temperature responses of the composite PCMs were recorded by an infrared thermal imager (Pti120, Fluke, USA). A photo-thermal conversion test was evaluated under simulated sunlight provided by a sun 2000 solar simulator (Abet Technologies, USA) with an AM 1.5 filter. The light irradiation power was verified using a CEL-NP2000 optical power meter (Ceaulight, China). The sample was placed in a foam box with the size (1.5 × 1.5 × 0.4 cm3) and irradiated under the simulated sunlight. Then, temperature-time variation curves of samples were obtained by using a multichannel temperature recorder (RX6000F, Mecon, China) at a step of 1 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structural of CDs and CW-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In our case, a simple and straightforward microwave-assisted method was used to prepare CDs, and the morphology of CDs was investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the TEM image revealed a large quantity of quasi-spherical nanostructures without noticeable aggregation at room temperature. The size of CDs was mainly distributed in the range of 1.3–4 nm with an average diameter of about 2.4 nm, these particles in nanoscale dimension can be readily incorporated into the channel of CW at room temperature and atmospheric pressure. Then, the FTIR and XPS were carried out to examine functional groups and chemical composition of the CDs. The FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. 2c reveals that the facile synthesized CDs contained abundant oxygen functional groups, such as O–H (3430 cm−1), C–O–C (1100 cm−1). Moreover, due to the efficient nitrogen doping effect as the CDs synthesis reaction proceed, various nitrogen-containing groups including amino (3245 cm−1) and amide species (1410 cm−1) were introduced on the surface of xylose-derived CDs. These FTIR allocations were further verified by XPS analysis. For instance, Fig. S1 indicated that the CDs was mainly composed of 73.59% C, 10.29% O and 16.12% N elements, the relative high atom concentration of O and N suggested the presence of abundant polar functional groups. Further, the high-resolution C 1s, O 1s and N 1s spectra revealed the surface-contained C–O, CO, C–OH, C–O–C, CN, C–N and N–H groups in addition to the CC/C–C chemical species that was the prime skeleton of carbon cores. For the prepared CW, only C and O elements can be determined from XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2d). Besides, the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) showed that the CC bond was the primary composition of this carbonized 3D framework. By contrast, the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2f) analysis for the CW-CDs scaffold proved the existence of N element with a relative high content (8.59%) due to the incorporation of N-doped CDs. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) could be fitted with four components centered at 284.4, 285.3, 286.2, and 287.8 eV, corresponding to the CC/C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum displayed three peaks at 531.2, 532.5 and 534 eV, which could be attributed to CO, C–OH/C–O–C, and H2O, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2i) exhibited the distinctive peaks at 398.7, 399.3 eV, and 400.2, representing pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively [30,31]. Hence, the XPS spectra indicated that CW was decorated with CDs efficiently by simple absorption, which consistent with the analysis of FTIR. Moreover, the EDX mapping of CW and CW-CDs indicated that the CW had a homogeneous and high density of C signal, but the CW-CDs not only had high density of C signal, but also had a homogeneous and low density of N signal (Fig. S2). To sum up, the CDs had been successfully incorporated in channels of carbonized wood, making it more possible form H-bonding with PEG and prevent PEG leakage effectively. The hydrogen bond formation could be further confirmed by observation from Fig. S3 that the –OH stretching peak shifts to lower wavenumbers (from 3434 cm−1 of PEG to 3410 cm−1 of CDs-PEG) [32,33], and the resultant noncovalent bond interaction favors the preparation of shape-stabilized composite PCMs.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of the cross and longitudinal sections of CW, CW-CDs, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG under different magnifications. As seen in Fig. 3a and b, CW and CW-CDs not only exhibited abundant pore structure but also retained the straight channels of natural wood along the growth direction after carbonization. Notably, the well-aligned micro-/mesoporous could be clearly observed in the cross section. Beyond that, it could be found that the pits with high density distributed homogeneously in the longitudinal section. These straight channels and pits could perform as a pathway, which is conducive to absorbing and transporting molten PEG for the fabrication of shape-stabilized composite PCMs [34]. After PEG loading, it could be seen that the CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG had smooth surface morphology (Fig. 3c and d). Meanwhile, the straight channels and pore structure of both CW and CW-CDs were filled with PEG and became less visible, suggesting that the PEG were successfully impregnated into the porous structure of CW and CW-CDs. In addition, it was also observed that the resultant encapsulation of CW and CW-CDs showed negligible difference, thus confirming that the hierarchical porous structure of CW was free from the effect of nano-sized CDs. These characterizations confirmed that the three-dimensional porous carbon framework of both CW and CW-CDs can not only provide mechanical support for composite PCMs, but also possess excellent adsorption properties for PEG.

The shape stability performance of organic PCMs is an important evaluation indicator in practical thermal-storage applications [35]. Therefore, the leakage test was performed at 80 °C for 6 h to validate the shape stability of pure PEG, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the solid pure PEG was completely melted after heating for 15 min, the liquid PEG spread out on the filter paper. While for the CW-PEG, there was only a faint exudation blot on the filter paper after 15 min (Fig. 4b). Notably, the CW-CDs-PEG maintained its original shape without any liquid leakage that can be found on the paper (Fig. 4c). To further compare the shape stability of CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG as the time prolongs, the leakage situation and weight loss of PEG were recorded. After 6 h recording, the leakage phenomenon of CW-PEG became more apparent, while the CW-CDs-PEG still exhibited outstanding shape stability as the same as the case of initial heating, the mass loss fraction of PEG in CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG were illustrated in Fig. S4. Overall, the leakage of CW-PEG was 6.15%, indicating that the porous structure could prevent the leakage of PEG during the melting process by surface tension force and capillary action [36,37]. Hence, the CW-PEG possessed excellent shape stability compared with the pure PEG, whereas the CW-CDs-PEG showed negligibly mass loss of PEG (PEG with only 0.98% mass loss may come from the its melted state on the sample surface), and exhibited better shape stability than the CW-PEG. It is reasonable to deduce that PEG molecules can be anchored well into the CW-based framework via H-bonding interactions with the surface hydroxyl and amine functional group on the CDs. Meanwhile, the porous structure also can prevent the leakage of PEG in the melting process through capillarity and surface tension. Accordingly, the CW-CDs-PEG possessed excellent shape-stabilized property even after long period utilization, and which in turn suggest that the CW-CDs was an ideal supporting material for encapsulation of organic PCMs.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore-size distribution were detected to explore the porosity of the CW and CW-CDs. These two samples exhibited a typical type-IV N2 isotherm with a distinct hysteresis loop at P/P0 = 0.4–1.0, while continuous rise of the isotherms below the relative pressure (P/P0) of 0.1 (Fig. 5 a), suggesting the coexistence of both microspores and mesoporous in the CW and CW-CDs [38]. This result was consistent with the pore-size distribution pattern (Fig. 5b), it is clear that the pore size was in the range of 2–50 nm, and the major dimension was centered at around 3 nm. Such porous structure conducive to impregnating PEG, because micro/meso-pore had a physically powerful capillary force [39]. Furthermore, the surface areas and pore parameters of the two samples were summarized in Table S1. CW had a high surface area of 580.8 m2 g−1, while the surface area was decreased to 451.2 m2 g−1 after incorporation of CDs. The major reason for this fact was that the nano-sized CDs was loaded into the pore of the CW by physical attachment, thereby reducing the surface area of CW-CDs. Nevertheless, such a small change of surface area could not affect the adsorption effect of CW-CDs, and the corresponding results could be verified from the SEM images.

To examine the crystal structure of pure PEG and the composite PCMs, the XRD patterns were listed in Fig. 5c. The CW and CW-CDs exhibited two broad diffraction peaks at 22° and 44°, corresponding to the (002) and (101) reflections of graphitic carbon (Fig. S5a), respectively [40], indicating that the degree of carbonization of CW was relatively high. After PEG impregnation, CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG showed a high crystalline state that were similar to that of pure PEG. There are two very strong diffraction peaks at 2θ of 19.04° and 23.38°, which represents two crystal planes of (001) and (110) [41]. Thus, the CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG proved all the characteristic diffraction peaks of pure PEG, suggesting that there were no chemical reactions between the supporting material (CW and CW-CDs) and the PEG, and the crystal structure of the PEG was not altered or destroyed after the encapsulation process. These XRD results were further verified by FTIR analysis. As shown in Fig. 5d, the FTIR spectra of CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG exhibited several new peaks at 2886, 963, 1468, 1286, and 832 cm−1 in comparison with CW and CW-CDs (Fig. S5b), which can be ascribed to the existences of –CH2 and C–H bands from the PEG. Moreover, the peak intensity of C–O–C at 1100 cm−1 significantly increased due to the introduction of PEG. It was observed that all absorption peaks of pure PEG appeared in the CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG was almost same, thus further confirming that the PEG was only physically absorbed into the porous structure of both CW and CW-CDs [42,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase change performances of composite PCMs
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal energy storage properties of CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG, which was determined based on the thermophysical parameters (phase change temperatures and enthalpies), were investigated by DSC analysis. Fig. 6 displayed the DSC thermograms of pure PEG, CW-PEG, CW-CDs-PEG, CW-PEG-50 and CW-CDs-PEG-50, and the corresponding thermal characteristics were summarized in Table 1. As shown in Fig. 6a, pure PEG showed an endothermic peak and an exothermic peak, which corresponds to the melting phase transition and the crystallization phase transition, respectively. As similar to the pure PEG, the CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG also had almost the same curved shapes, the major reason is highly related to the fact that only physical adsorption and H-bond were responsible for the PEG loading. Therefore, the two composite PCMs presented the same phase transition behavior with pure PEG. To future research the reliability of CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG, 50 heating-cooling cycles were implemented. As shown in Fig. 6b and c, the two composite PCMs exhibited almost identical change behavior before and after thermal cycling, indicating that the CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG can still maintain an excellent phase transition performance even after 50 cycles.

As summarized in Table 1, the melting and crystallization temperature of pure PEG were 57.4 °C and 37.5 °C, respectively, and the corresponding the melting and crystallization enthalpy were 183.3 J/g and 177.1 J/g, respectively, illustrating that PEG possessed favorable phase change behavior and high latent heat. However, CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG exhibited a reduction in latent heat compared with the pure PEG, the result could also be intuitively obtained in Fig. 6d. The main reason was that the supporting materials fabricated from CW and CW-CDs had no phase transition performance, thus, it was normal that the enthalpy value of the sample was lower than that of pure PEG. On the other hand, the impregnation ratio (R) of supporting materials (CW and CW-CDs) was limited (Table 2 ), showing 71.9% and 61.9% R value for CW and CW-CDs, respectively. Furthermore, since the incorporated CDs led to the decrease of R value, the latent heat decreased from 130.5 J/g for CW-PEG to 112.4 J/g for CW-CDs-PEG. Despite this issue, the heat storage capacity with value more than 100 J/g, which together with excellent shape stability, can provide great potential for efficient thermal-to-energy conversion and storage. Qian and co-workers reported that the increasing interaction between supporting materials and organic PCMs would result in a lower transition temperature of composite PCMs [44]. In our case, the transition temperature of CW-CDs-PEG was lower that of CW-PEG, it is likely that more intense interaction could be generated owing to the CDs-induced H-bonding and increased capillary force. In addition, the supercooling temperature of the pure PEG was 19.9 °C, whereas the supercooling temperature of CW-CDs-PEG (15.3 °C) was lower than that of the pure PEG (by 4.6 °C) in Fig. S6. It is presumed that the porous carbon supporting scaffold, which suffered high degree of carbonization and possessed excellent compatibility with PEG, provides a highly thermal conduction road and thus sped up the phase transition of PCMs. In addition, it was noteworthy that the enthalpy values and phase change temperature of CW-CDs-PEG had no obvious change after 50 heating-cooling cycles, manifesting that the CW-CDs-PEG had good recyclability. Based on the above-mentioned analysis, the as-prepared CW-CDs-PEG possessed high latent and excellent thermal reliability as a novel thermal energy storage system.

The thermal conductivity of PCMs is positively associated with the rate of energy storage and release in thermal energy storage system [45]. Fig. 7 illustrates the thermal conductivity of pure PEG, CW, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG at radial direction and at 25 °C. The CW had a higher thermal conductivity (0.329 W/m K) compared with the wood (0.101 W/m K), because the lignocellulosic components of wood were converted into carbon-based 3D framework via a high degree of carbonization, this change favors the thermal transport based on the intense thermal vibration of the phonon in graphitized carbon [46]. Hence, the thermal conductivity of CW-PEG composite was up to 0.432 W/m K, which was 109% higher than that of the pure PEG. Previous report has demonstrated that introducing functional groups like amine could play an important role in reducing the band gaps of supporting materials, and enhancing the thermal conductivity of the composite materials [47]. For the prepared composite PCMs in our research, the thermal conductivity value was increased from 0.432 for CW-PEG to 0.449 W/m K for CW-CDs-PEG, because numerous amine groups were attached on surface of N-doped CDs that were loaded in the porous structure of CW.

Fig. 8 shows the TGA and the corresponding DTG curves of pure PEG, CW, CW-CDs, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG. Clearly, CW and CW-CDs had a little weight loss and almost no degradation from 25 °C to 700 °C (Fig. 8a), validating that the CW and CW-CDs were stable and appropriate for using as a supporting material. But for pure PEG, the shape thermal degradation can be observed within the temperature range of 300–460 °C. Thus, only one peak appeared in the corresponding DTG curve, and the maximum weight loss rate appeared at 402 °C (Fig. 8b). Additionally, it was clearly found that the CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG exhibited the same degradation process with pure PEG, and the mass loss was occurred when the temperature increased from 300 to 460 °C. The total weight loss percentages of pure PEG, CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG was 100%, 76.9%, and 67.6%, respectively, which may be attributed to the thermal decomposition of PEG in the composites. It was noteworthy that the weight loss percentages were consistent with the encapsulation ratio of pure PEG into the CW and CW-CDs, indicating that the prepared CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG composite PCMs is homogeneous, thereby, it makes a favorable condition for improving thermal stability for solar energy conversion and storage.

The photo-thermal conversion behavior of pure PEG, CW, CW-CDs, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG were investigated under a simulated solar illumination (AM 1.5) with an intensity of 150 mW cm−2. The temperature variations inside the samples were recorded by a paperless recorder equipped with thermocouples, as illustrated in Fig. 9 a. The temperature evolution curves with or without the simulated solar illumination were shown in Fig. 9b. Under solar radiation, the temperature increasing speed of CW-CDs-PEG was apparently higher than that of other samples, and this behavior outperformed to that of pure PEG. It was due to the fact that the supporting material of CW-CDs had effective absorption in wide wavelength range, which could be demonstrated by the ultraviolet–visible absorption curves (Fig. S7). Immobilization of the CDs within CW led to the complete fluorescence quenching; consequently, the energy absorbed by the CDs upon irradiation likely dissipates only through nonradiative process, giving rise to photothermal conversion and subsequent temperature increase. Moreover, CW-CDs-PEG could simultaneously achieve photo-thermal energy conversion and thermal energy storage, which was due to the optical property of CW-CDs and the latent energy storage of PEG. Therefore, the temperature increased more significant for CW-CDs-PEG. As the irradiation time increased, the melted PEG in CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG led to the reduced slope and inflection point appear, demonstrating that the occurrence of the solid−liquid phase transition of the CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG started at ∼53 °C. After the light was shut down, the temperature of all samples decreased sharply. However, the samples of CW-PEG and CW-CDs-PEG exhibited obvious solidification platform at ∼31 °C, implying that the latent heat was released as the solidification of PEG in the composites. Nonetheless, pure PEG had almost no phase change platform during heating and cooling process for, the underlying reason was that pure PEG in white color can reflect the irradiating light and thus lead to the inferior light absorption. As a consequence, the highest temperature of pure PEG was ∼48 °C within the same irradiation time duration, indicating that there is no phase transition in the pure PEG. Furthermore, the photo-thermal conversion and storage efficiency (η) could be determined by the following eq. (1) [48].
(1)
η =
m Δ H
ρ S
(
t t
−
t f
)
× 100 %
Where m is the weight of the sample,
Δ H
represents the phase change enthalpy, ρ stands for the optical power density of simulated light source, S represents the surface area of the sample,
t t
and
t f
are the starting and terminating time before and after the phase change, respectively. According to the eq. (1), the η of CW-CDs-PEG (84.4%) was much higher than that of CW-PEG (79.7%), the result was consistent with the above temperature change curve. Based on above-mentioned analysis, the superior photo-thermal conversion performance of CW-CDs-PEG will afford a new choice for solar energy conversion utilization in actual application.

To future investigate the heat transfer rate and thermal management capability of samples during heating and cooling, the surface temperature changes of pure PEG, CW, CW-PEG, and CW-CDs-PEG were recorded by using an infrared thermal image. As shown in Fig. 9c, the color change of CW-CDs-PEG was the most significant within the same time duration (100 s), indicating its higher heat transfer rate. During the cooling process (Fig. 9d), the surface temperature of CW-CDs-PEG was still higher than that of other samples. This was mainly because the CW-CDs-PEG could release heat during cooling, thus preventing the decrease of the surface temperature. The most typical feature was that the naturally cooling duration of CW-CDs-PEG was about 14 min (Fig. 9e). These results suggested that the CW-CDs-PEG had fast heat transfer rate and good heat storage performance during heating or cooling process. Specially, after heating for 100 s, the shape of pure PEG had taken place visibly variation as the PEG melt into liquid. However, the CW-CDs-PEG still exhibited excellent shape stability. To sum up, the CW-CDs-PEG demonstrated favorable capacity of solar harvesting and thermal converting.

In summary, a novel shape-stabilized composite PCMs was prepared by innovatively using CW loaded with CDs as the supporting material of PEG. The porous structure of CW-CDs and surface polarity functional groups of CDs are superior in preventing the leakage of PEG during the heating process, while the interconnected carbon skeleton of CW could promote the thermal conductivity. Hence, the cost-effective PCMs derived from CW-CDs-PEG composite showed excellent shape-stabilized property, and the thermal conductivity (0.449 W/m K) was 117% higher than that of pure PEG. Furthermore, the CW-CDs-PEG also exhibited efficient photo-thermal conversion efficiency (84.4%), large energy storage capacity (112.4 J/g), good thermal reliability after 50 heating-cooling cycles, and favorable thermal stability. Benefiting from green preparation strategy, encapsulation capacity, and excellent thermal properties, the CW-CDs-PEG composite prepared in this work will be a promising candidate that can be used as solar water heating systems. Further, the ongoing work in our lab is investigating shape-stabilized composite PCMs with greater thermal conductivity.

Xinghui Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ziqi Zhu: Investigation. Pei Yang: Investigation. Zhenping You: Investigation. Yue Dong: Investigation. Miao Tang: Investigation. Minzhi Chen: Conceptualization. Xiaoyan Zhou: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31870549), and the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University.

Yang, Largely enhanced thermal conductivity of poly (ethylene glycol)/boron nitride composite phase change materials for solar-thermal-electric energy conversion and storage with very low content of graphene nanoplatelets, Chem. Eng. J. 315(2017) 481-490.
